scenario.architecture_name: He performs rare-provider charm early, but already talks like my time and reactions should answer his standards.
scenario.demographics: F/22 Ghanaian immigrant, first-gen college student, part-time job; M/26 Ghanaian immigrant
scenario.cultural_context: African immigrant family expectations for marriage; first-gen daughterly duty; community gatherings over individual celebrations; pressure to marry within the community
core_behaviors: Frames my hesitation as me needing to keep up with his standards
Takes over plans in a way that feels caring now
scenario.satellite_signals: Community respectability, marriage talk, and little tests about how I act, all wrapped in him sounding like he knows what he wants.
scenario.dismissible_red_flags: His questions can sound like normal courting and seriousness, and I do like that he seems intentional.
scenario.scenario_text:
I met Kojo through my cousin’s church group in Dorchester, and at first he just seemed like a grown man, which was honestly a relief. He’s 26, works in IT, and always has this calm way of talking like he already knows the answer. Last week he offered to pick me up after my shift at the coffee shop, and he brought my favorite kelewele from a place near Nubian Square, which was sweet. He said, “I’m protecting my legacy, not wasting time,” and I laughed because it sounded dramatic, but also kind of nice, like he was serious about something for once.

My aunties liked that he came to the family cookout early and helped carry the chairs out before anyone asked. He kept calling me my queen, and in front of everybody he said I was “different from the girls around here” because I stay focused on school and work. I know he meant it as a compliment, and it did make me feel chosen, especially since my mom has been reminding me that girls our age should not be playing around if they want a good husband.

It was just a little thing, but when I told him I had a study group Saturday, he said, “You should be lucky to have me, so don’t disappear on me.” He smiled after, like he was joking, and then he added that he only dates women who know how to act right. I didn’t really know what to say, so I just said, “Okay, Mr. Standards,” and he laughed and squeezed my hand.

He’s been so sweet otherwise, always checking if I ate, always asking if my uncle in Accra would approve, and saying he wants something “solid” and “community-minded.” I like that he talks about marriage like it’s normal, not some fantasy. But the “act right” comment stayed with me a little. Was that just him teasing, or was it weird?

snippets.snippet_text: I met Kojo through my cousin’s church group in Dorchester
snippets.reasoning: This places her inside a Ghanaian immigrant community in Boston and shows the relationship starting through family and church, not a random dating app.
A tidy status marker. His job and age make him sound settled and intentional, which helps him occupy the adult, deciding position early.
This is the first small signal that his certainty matters more than her view. It reads as confidence now, but it positions him as the authority.

snippets.snippet_text: picked me up after my shift at the coffee shop
snippets.reasoning: He controls the movement and timing while she comes from work. It’s helpful on the surface, but it also sets up him as the one who handles logistics.
He’s using a culturally specific food in a thoughtful way, which is affectionate. At the same time, it creates emotional debt because she feels specially seen and lucky.

snippets.snippet_text: I’m protecting my legacy, not wasting time
snippets.reasoning: This is self-declaration language that sounds serious and principled. In context it also frames him as a man whose priorities outrank anyone else’s comfort or pace.

snippets.snippet_text: My aunties liked that he came to the family cookout early
snippets.reasoning: This shows he knows how to move inside her family space and win approval quickly. It’s ordinary hospitality on the surface, but it builds his access to the people who matter to her.

snippets.snippet_text: helped carry the chairs out before anyone asked
snippets.reasoning: A helpful gesture, but it’s also an early competence display. He moves first, takes over, and becomes the man who does things before others can even ask.
This is affectionate and flattering, but it also lifts her into a role he names. The praise feels warm because it sounds special, not generic.

snippets.snippet_text: different from the girls around here
snippets.reasoning: He elevates her by putting down other women in the area. That kind of compliment makes her feel chosen now, but it quietly rewards separation from her peers.
This reflects the family-marriage expectation she already lives under. It also shows how easily the relationship can be folded into duty and respectability.

snippets.snippet_text: You should be lucky to have me
snippets.reasoning: This is the clearest status-claim line. It sounds playful in the moment, but it installs a hierarchy where gratitude is expected from her.
What sounds like a joke is also a claim on her availability. He is already treating normal scheduling as something she owes him.

snippets.snippet_text: only dates women who know how to act right
snippets.reasoning: This is a direct behavioral test dressed up as standards. If she objects later, he can say he told her upfront what kind of woman he expects.
Her reply shows she’s smoothing over a line that landed a little hard. She converts his demand into a joke so the moment stays light.
This is caring behavior, and it will feel good to someone far from home. It also starts building a rhythm where his attentiveness becomes part of the bond she has to maintain.

snippets.snippet_text: asking if my uncle in Accra would approve
snippets.reasoning: He’s speaking the language of family duty and community approval, which she takes seriously. That makes his interest feel respectful, but it also gives him a foothold inside her obligations.
These are virtue words in her world. They make his seriousness sound admirable while quietly tying his expectations to community norms she can’t easily question.

snippets.snippet_text: the “act right” comment stayed with me a little
snippets.reasoning: She is not alarmed, but she notices the line lingered. That means the praise and the control are already arriving together, even if she can’t name it yet.
